metadata.title: UPSC Mains GS Paper 2 — Social Justice Comprehensive Syllabus
metadata.purpose: Standalone Social Justice file for MentorsDaily UPSC portal — AI module generation
metadata.note: Separated from Governance file — Social Justice is a distinct GS2 component covering welfare, rights, and inclusion

book_1.title: Indian Society
book_1.author: NCERT Class 12 Sociology
book_1.usage: Social stratification, caste, gender, poverty frameworks

book_2.title: Economic Survey (Annual)
book_2.usage: Latest data on social sector spending, schemes performance

book_3.title: India Year Book
book_3.usage: Scheme details, beneficiary data, ministry initiatives

book_4.title: 2nd ARC Report 13
book_4.usage: Policy framework for SC, ST, OBC, minorities, disabled

book_5.title: 2nd ARC Report 14
book_5.usage: Child rights, child labour, juvenile justice framework

modules.module_name: Welfare Schemes for Vulnerable Sections — Overview and Framework

topics.topic_name: Welfare State Concept and India's Social Protection Architecture
topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 13; Economic Survey Social Sector chapter
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Sociology Ch 5 — Patterns of Social Inequality and Exclusion
key_concepts: Welfare state defined — state responsibility for citizens' basic needs
Universal vs targeted welfare — debate on coverage vs cost efficiency
India's federal welfare structure — Centre, State, and Local Body roles
Centrally Sponsored Schemes (CSS) vs Central Sector Schemes distinction
Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) — Aadhaar-linked benefit delivery, JAM trinity
JAM Trinity — Jan Dhan, Aadhaar, Mobile as delivery infrastructure
PFMS — Public Financial Management System for fund tracking
Social audit — MGNREGA model, community verification of works
Convergence approach — POSHAN, Mission Shakti integrating multiple schemes
Aspirational Districts Programme — 112 backward districts focus
PM-GATI SHAKTI — infrastructure for last-mile welfare delivery
Niti Aayog's role in scheme design vs old Planning Commission approach
Outcome-based monitoring — moving from input to output to outcome measurement
Decentralisation of welfare — PRIs as delivery vehicles under 73rd/74th Amendment
International benchmarks — SDG 1 (No Poverty), SDG 2 (Zero Hunger) India's position

scheme_1.name: PM-JAY (Ayushman Bharat)
scheme_1.objective: Health insurance of Rs 5 lakh per family to bottom 40% households

scheme_2.name: PMAY-G (Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana — Gramin)
scheme_2.objective: 2.95 crore pucca houses to rural homeless by 2024

scheme_3.name: MGNREGA
scheme_3.objective: 100 days guaranteed wage employment to rural households

issues_challenges: Leakages in welfare delivery despite DBT — ghost beneficiaries persist
Exclusion errors — genuine beneficiaries left out due to documentation
Low awareness among intended beneficiaries especially in remote areas
Scheme fatigue — too many overlapping schemes causing confusion
Inadequate grievance redressal for welfare scheme denials
reforms_needed: Universal Basic Income pilot — cash transfers replacing multiple schemes
Single beneficiary database — de-duplication across all schemes
Strengthen social audit institutions with independent statutory backing
Improve last-mile delivery through trained frontline workers
Regular impact evaluation by independent agencies

topics.topic_name: Food Security — PDS, NFSA, Nutrition Schemes
topics.primary_source: Economic Survey; India Year Book; NFSA 2013 text
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 9 Economics Ch 4 — Food Security in India
key_concepts: NFSA 2013 — National Food Security Act — legal entitlement to subsidised food
Coverage: 75% rural, 50% urban population — 81+ crore beneficiaries
Antodaya Anna Yojana — poorest of poor — 35 kg per family per month
Priority Households — 5 kg per person per month at Re 1-3
PDS evolution — universal to targeted (TPDS) to rights-based (NFSA)
FCI — Food Corporation of India — procurement, storage, distribution
MSP and procurement — how food enters PDS system
Leakages in PDS — diversion of grain, ghost cards, quality issues
One Nation One Ration Card (ONORC) — portability for migrant workers
PM Garib Kalyan Anna Yojana — free grain during COVID and extended
Mid-Day Meal Scheme (PM POSHAN) — school nutrition, 12 crore children
ICDS (Integrated Child Development Services) — anganwadi nutrition
POSHAN Abhiyan (National Nutrition Mission) — stunting, wasting targets
Fortification of food — rice, wheat, oil fortification policy
Global Hunger Index — India's ranking and credibility debate

scheme_1.name: PM POSHAN (Mid-Day Meal)
scheme_1.objective: Hot cooked meals to 11.8 crore school children

scheme_2.name: POSHAN Abhiyan
scheme_2.objective: Reduce stunting to 25%, undernutrition to 22% by 2022

scheme_3.name: ONORC
scheme_3.objective: Ration card portability across states for migrants

issues_challenges: Malnutrition coexists with food surplus — storage and distribution failure
NFSA beneficiary list outdated — exclusions and inclusions errors
Nutritional diversity lacking — PDS provides calories not micronutrients
Mid-day meal quality variability — states with poor implementation
Anganwadis understaffed and under-resourced
reforms_needed: Expand NFSA to include eggs and millets for nutritional diversity
Modernise FCI warehousing — reduce storage losses
Strengthen ONORC by including all states and better beneficiary verification
Integrate ICDS with health system for convergent nutrition outcomes
Community-based management of acute malnutrition (CMAM) scale-up

sample_questions: Examine the role of the JAM Trinity in transforming welfare delivery in India. (10 marks)
Critically evaluate the National Food Security Act 2013. Has it achieved its objectives? (12.5 marks)

topics.topic_name: Women's Rights, Safety, and Legal Framework
topics.primary_source: India Year Book Women chapter; Economic Survey Gender section
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 11 Sociology Ch 2 — Social Change and Social Order
key_concepts: PWDVA 2005 — Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act — civil and criminal remedies
IPC 498A — cruelty by husband and relatives — misuse debate
Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (POSH) Act 2013 — ICC mandate
Nirbhaya case 2012 — amendments, fast-track courts, death penalty for rape
Criminal Law Amendment Act 2013 — expanded definition of sexual assault
POCSO 2012 — child sexual abuse — gender-neutral provisions
Maternity Benefit Act 1961 amended 2017 — 26 weeks paid leave
Equal Remuneration Act 1976 — gender pay gap legal framework
Dowry Prohibition Act 1961 — persistent enforcement challenge
MTP Act 1971 amended 2021 — medical termination of pregnancy liberalised
Vishaka Guidelines 1997 — precursor to POSH Act
Triple Talaq criminalisation 2019 — Muslim Women Protection Act
Online safety for women — cyberstalking, revenge porn — IT Act provisions
Fast Track Special Courts (FTSCs) for POCSO and rape cases
Gender Budgeting — Gender Budget Statement in Union Budget since 2005

scheme_2.name: Beti Bachao Beti Padhao
scheme_2.objective: Address declining child sex ratio, girls' education

scheme_3.name: One Stop Centres (Sakhi)
scheme_3.objective: Integrated support for women victims of violence

issues_challenges: Low conviction rate in rape cases — secondary victimisation by system
POSH Act compliance poor in informal sector
Dowry deaths continue despite legal prohibition
Marital rape not criminalised — legal gap
Online violence against women rising rapidly
reforms_needed: Criminalise marital rape — Supreme Court reference pending
Strengthen witness protection for sexual assault survivors
Extend POSH Act coverage to domestic workers and informal workers
Increase women judges in fast-track courts
Digital literacy for online safety awareness

topics.topic_name: Women's Economic Empowerment — SHGs, Labour Force Participation
topics.primary_source: Economic Survey; NRLM programme documents; World Bank India gender reports
key_concepts: Female Labour Force Participation Rate (FLFPR) — India at ~24% vs world average 47%
FLFPR U-shaped hypothesis — declines with development then rises again
SHG movement — 89 lakh SHGs, 10 crore women members under DAY-NRLM
Bank linkage programme — SHGs as collateral-free credit channel
Kudumbashree (Kerala) vs SERP (Andhra) as SHG success models
Women entrepreneurship — MUDRA loans, Udyogini scheme
Gender pay gap — 19% gap (ILO data), causes and solutions
Occupational segregation — women concentrated in low-wage sectors
Unpaid care work — 8x more unpaid work by women (time use survey)
Maternity leave — 26 weeks vs global average, impact on employment
Flexi-work and work-from-home as gender equity tools
Women in agriculture — 80% of food production but land rights denied
Financial inclusion — PM Jan Dhan accounts — 55% women account holders
Skill India for women — PMKVY, Hunar Haat
Political representation — 33% reservation in local bodies vs Parliament gap

scheme_1.name: DAY-NRLM (Aajeevika)
scheme_1.objective: Organise rural poor women into SHGs for sustainable livelihoods

scheme_2.name: PM Mudra Yojana
scheme_2.objective: Collateral-free loans up to Rs 10 lakh for micro enterprises

issues_challenges: SHGs — credit reaching women but not always decision-making power
Women land rights — inheritance laws still discriminatory in practice
Childcare infrastructure absent — deters women workforce entry
Domestic violence inhibits economic participation
Digital gender divide — 40% less likely to own smartphone
reforms_needed: Universal childcare — creches at workplaces and public spaces
Joint land titling in rural housing schemes
Strengthen women's property rights in succession laws
Expand digital literacy for women entrepreneurs
Women's Reservation Bill passage in Parliament

sample_questions: Examine the role of Self-Help Groups in women's economic empowerment in India. (10 marks)
Critically analyse the legal framework for women's safety in India. Are laws sufficient? (12.5 marks)

topics.primary_source: RTE Act 2009 text; NEP 2020 document; Economic Survey Education chapter
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch 7 — Rights
key_concepts: Right to Education Act 2009 — Article 21A — free and compulsory education 6-14 years
25% reservation in private schools for EWS/disadvantaged — Section 12(1)(c)
RTE implementation gaps — teacher vacancies, infrastructure, quality concerns
Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) — Primary 100%+, Secondary 79%, Higher 28%
NEP 2020 — 5+3+3+4 structure replacing 10+2
Foundational literacy and numeracy — NIPUN Bharat target — Grade 3 by 2026-27
Mother tongue as medium of instruction till Class 5 (NEP 2020)
Multidisciplinary higher education — exit options, academic bank of credits
EKLAVYA Model Residential Schools — quality education for tribal children
Kasturba Gandhi Balika Vidyalayas — girls' residential schools
Samagra Shiksha Abhiyan — integrated school education programme
Annual Status of Education Report (ASER) — learning outcomes crisis data
Gender Parity Index — achieved at primary, gaps persist at secondary
NEET and higher education access — backward classes exclusion debate
Digital divide in education — post-COVID online learning inequity

scheme_1.name: Samagra Shiksha Abhiyan
scheme_1.objective: Holistic school education — access, equity, quality integrated

scheme_2.name: NIPUN Bharat
scheme_2.objective: Foundational literacy and numeracy for Grade 3 students by 2026-27

scheme_3.name: EKLAVYA Schools
scheme_3.objective: Quality residential education to tribal children in Schedule V areas

issues_challenges: Learning outcomes crisis — students in Class 5 cannot read Class 2 text
Teacher absenteeism and poor quality in government schools
Digital divide — 40% rural students lacked device access during COVID
Dropout rate at secondary level especially for girls and SC/ST
First-generation learners lack home support environment
reforms_needed: Full implementation of NEP 2020 with teacher retraining
Strengthen mother-tongue instruction with multilingual materials
Compulsory pre-school for all — universal ECCE by 2030
Reduce dropout through conditional cash transfers for secondary
Digital infrastructure in rural schools — PM e-VIDYA scale-up

sample_questions: Examine the key features of NEP 2020 and assess its potential to transform Indian education. (12.5 marks)
Despite high enrolment, learning outcomes remain poor in India. Analyse the causes and suggest remedies. (10 marks)

modules.module_name: Health and Nutrition — Social Justice Dimension

topics.topic_name: Universal Health Coverage and Health Schemes
topics.primary_source: NHM documents; Ayushman Bharat programme; NITI Aayog Health Index
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Sociology Ch 3 — Social Institutions: Continuity and Change
key_concepts: Universal Health Coverage (UHC) — WHO definition — financial protection + quality services
India's public health expenditure — 2.1% of GDP (National Health Policy 2017 target: 2.5%)
NHM — National Health Mission — NHM-R (Rural) + NHM-U (Urban)
Ayushman Bharat — PM-JAY — Rs 5 lakh cover, secondary and tertiary hospitalisation
Health and Wellness Centres (HWCs) — 1.5 lakh comprehensive primary care centres
ASHA workers — community health link, 10 lakh workers
IMR, MMR, U5MR — India's progress and remaining gaps
IMR declined from 68 (2000) to 28 (2022); MMR from 254 to 97
Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) — diabetes, cancer, hypertension rising
Mental health — NMHP, mental health act 2017, huge treatment gap (83%)
Out-of-pocket expenditure (OOPE) — 47% of total health expenditure — financial catastrophe
India's doctor-population ratio — 1:834 vs WHO norm 1:1000
AYUSH integration — traditional medicine mainstreaming
National Digital Health Mission (ABDM) — health ID, health records
COVID-19 lessons — ICU capacity, oxygen shortage, vaccine equity

scheme_1.name: Ayushman Bharat PM-JAY
scheme_1.objective: Rs 5 lakh health cover to 10.74 crore poor families

scheme_2.name: National Health Mission
scheme_2.objective: Universal healthcare access through strengthened public health system

scheme_3.name: POSHAN Abhiyan
scheme_3.objective: Convergent nutrition actions — reduce stunting by 2%, wasting by 2% annually

issues_challenges: High OOPE pushes 6 crore into poverty every year due to health costs
Urban-rural and state-level health infrastructure disparity
Health workforce shortage especially in primary care
Mental health neglect — less than 1% health budget
Rising NCD burden with inadequate lifestyle disease prevention infrastructure
reforms_needed: Increase public health spending to 2.5% GDP as per NHP 2017 target
Expand PM-JAY to primary care — not just hospitalisation
Compulsory rural posting for medical graduates
National mental health programme scale-up with community workers
Strengthen district hospitals as secondary care backbone

sample_questions: Universal Health Coverage remains elusive in India. Analyse the challenges and suggest a roadmap. (12.5 marks)
What is Ayushman Bharat? Evaluate its impact on healthcare access for the poor. (10 marks)

topics.primary_source: Economic Survey Poverty chapter; NITI Aayog MPI 2023; World Bank India reports
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 9 Economics Ch 3 — Poverty as Challenge
key_concepts: Absolute poverty — income/consumption below subsistence level
Relative poverty — below societal average — used in developed countries
Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI) — UNDP — health, education, living standards
India MPI 2023 — 415 million escaped poverty in 15 years (2005-2021)
Tendulkar Committee (2009) — poverty line based on consumption basket
Rangarajan Committee (2014) — higher poverty line — 29.5% BPL rural, 26.4% urban
SECC 2011 — Socio-Economic Caste Census — identifies BPL households for targeting
Human Poverty Index vs MPI — evolution of measurement
Social exclusion — not just income poverty but denial of rights, identity, voice
Feminisation of poverty — women disproportionately affected
Inter-generational poverty trap — poor children stay poor without education
Geographical concentration of poverty — BIMARU states, tribal districts
Urban poverty — slums, housing, sanitation, livelihoods
Vulnerable groups — SC, ST, minorities, disabled, single women, elderly
SDG 1 — end extreme poverty by 2030 — India's trajectory

scheme_1.name: MGNREGA
scheme_1.objective: 100-day employment guarantee as poverty safety net

scheme_2.name: PM SVANidhi
scheme_2.objective: Street vendors — collateral-free working capital loan

scheme_3.name: Aspirational Districts Programme
scheme_3.objective: Convergent development in 112 most backward districts

issues_challenges: Poverty line controversy — politically determined vs needs-based
Urban poverty undercounted — slum dwellers invisible to official statistics
Poverty and caste correlation — SC/ST over-represented
Climate vulnerability increasing poverty — floods, droughts
Exclusion from formal welfare — undocumented migrants, nomadic tribes
reforms_needed: Adopt MPI as official measure alongside income poverty
Universal social registry — single database of vulnerable households
Strengthen urban safety nets — MGNREGA equivalent for urban poor
Climate-adaptive livelihoods for weather-dependent poor
Social protection portability across states for migrant workers

sample_questions: Examine the Multidimensional Poverty Index and how it improves upon income-based poverty measurement. (10 marks)
Social exclusion is more than income poverty. Discuss with reference to vulnerable groups in India. (12.5 marks)

modules.module_name: SC/ST Welfare, Anti-Discrimination and Tribal Rights

topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch 5 — Rights
key_concepts: Articles 15(4) and 16(4) — affirmative action provisions for SC/ST/OBC
Article 46 — DPSP — state to promote SC/ST educational and economic interests
Article 335 — maintaining efficiency while making reservations
Mandal Commission 1980 — OBC reservation 27% — implemented 1990
Indra Sawhney v Union of India 1992 — 50% cap, no reservation in promotions
M. Nagaraj case 2006 — data on inadequacy required for SC/ST promotion reservation
EWS reservation 2019 — 10% for economically weaker sections from unreserved
Creamy layer concept — excludes well-off OBC from benefits
National Commission for SC and ST — constitutional bodies under Articles 338, 338A
SC/ST (Prevention of Atrocities) Act 1989 amended 2015, 2018 — special courts
Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes Orders — Presidential Orders
Manual scavenging — Prohibition of Manual Scavenging Act 2013
SCSP (SC Sub Plan) and TSP (Tribal Sub Plan) — 15%/7.5% budget allocation
Post-Matric Scholarship for SC/ST — education support
Rohith Vemula incident — caste discrimination in higher education debate

scheme_2.name: PM Anusuchit Jati Abhyuday Yojana
scheme_2.objective: Umbrella scheme for SC welfare — education, housing, livelihood

scheme_3.name: Van Dhan Yojana
scheme_3.objective: Tribal livelihood through value addition of Minor Forest Produce

issues_challenges: Atrocities continue — NCRB data shows rising SC/ST crime rates
Manual scavenging persists despite prohibition — systemic failure
Creamy layer for SC/ST demanded by many — opposed by others
Reservation benefits concentrated among dominant sub-castes within SC/OBC
Caste discrimination in premier educational institutions
reforms_needed: Sub-categorisation within SC/ST — Supreme Court constitution bench decision 2024
Expand reservation to private sector — debate on constitutional amendment
Strengthen special courts for Atrocities Act — fast disposal
Full abolition of manual scavenging with alternative livelihood and rehabilitation
Data-driven review of reservation — census caste data needed

topics.topic_name: Tribal Rights — Forest Rights, PESA, Land Acquisition
topics.primary_source: Forest Rights Act 2006; PESA 1996; LARR Act 2013
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Sociology Ch 5 — Patterns of Social Inequality and Exclusion
key_concepts: Forest Rights Act 2006 — individual and community forest rights
CFR (Community Forest Rights) — gram sabha as rights-holding body
FRA implementation status — titles granted vs pending — 47 lakh titles
PESA 1996 — Panchayats Extension to Scheduled Areas — tribal self-governance
5th Schedule areas — Governor's special powers, Tribes Advisory Council
6th Schedule — autonomous district councils in tribal Northeast
Land acquisition of tribal land — safeguards in LARR 2013
Schedule V land alienation laws — restrictions on transfer to non-tribals
Displacement and rehabilitation — tribal population 40% of all displaced
Niyamgiri judgment 2013 — gram sabha consent in mining on tribal land
Vedanta case — industry vs tribal rights landmark
TRIFED — tribal cooperative for forest produce marketing
Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Groups (PVTGs) — 75 groups
PM JANMAN scheme 2023 — PVTGs development mission
Tribal education — Eklavya schools, Ashram schools quality concerns

scheme_1.name: PM JANMAN
scheme_1.objective: Saturation of 11 critical interventions for 75 PVTG communities

scheme_2.name: Forest Rights Act Implementation
scheme_2.objective: Recognise individual and community forest rights of tribals

issues_challenges: FRA implementation — titles rejected arbitrarily, CFR underutilised
Mining displacement continues without proper rehabilitation
PESA states laws — most states yet to pass conformity legislation
Traditional knowledge of tribals unprotected against biopiracy
PVTGs face extinction risk — small population, high disease burden
reforms_needed: Complete FRA implementation — clear backlog of pending titles
Mandatory gram sabha consent for all projects in Schedule V areas
PESA conformity legislation in all 10 Fifth Schedule states
Dedicated tribal university in each tribal-heavy state
PM JANMAN full funding and timely implementation for PVTGs

sample_questions: Examine the Forest Rights Act 2006 as an instrument of social justice for tribal communities. (12.5 marks)
Discuss the constitutional safeguards for Scheduled Castes. Are they adequate to address caste discrimination? (10 marks)

topics.topic_name: Child Rights Framework — POCSO, JJ Act, Child Labour Laws
topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 14; NCPCR guidelines; Ministry of WCD reports
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 11 Sociology Ch 3 — Social Institutions
key_concepts: UNCRC 1989 — UN Convention on Rights of the Child — India ratified 1992
POCSO Act 2012 — Protection of Children from Sexual Offences — gender neutral
Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection) Act 2015 — amended 2021
JJ Act — child in conflict with law vs child in need of care distinction
JJBs (Juvenile Justice Boards) — quasi-judicial for children in conflict with law
CWCs (Child Welfare Committees) — administrative protection for children in need
Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Amendment Act 2016
Complete ban on labour below 14; hazardous industry ban below 18
CHILDLINE 1098 — emergency helpline for children
ICPS — Integrated Child Protection Scheme
Missing children — TrackChild portal, Khoya Paya portal
Child trafficking — ITPA (Immoral Traffic Prevention Act) provisions
Adoption — CARA (Central Adoption Resource Authority) — Hague Convention
Street children — Smile Foundation, Bachpan Bachao Andolan
NCPCR — National Commission for Protection of Child Rights — statutory body

scheme_1.name: Mission Vatsalya (earlier ICPS)
scheme_1.objective: Child protection — institutionalisation reduction, family-based care

scheme_2.name: POCSO e-Box
scheme_2.objective: Online complaint mechanism for child sexual abuse

issues_challenges: Low conviction rate under POCSO — lengthy trials, hostile witnesses
Child labour in brick kilns, agriculture, domestic work — enforcement gap
Cybercrime against children rising — child pornography, online grooming
JJ homes — institutional abuse documented by NHRC, NCPCR
Child trafficking — weak inter-state coordination
reforms_needed: Fast-track POCSO courts in every district
Independent inspection of JJ homes and child care institutions
Mandatory reporting of child abuse by teachers, doctors
Child-friendly police stations and courts
Awareness campaigns on CHILDLINE in rural areas

sample_questions: Evaluate the effectiveness of the POCSO Act in protecting children from sexual offences. (10 marks)
Despite legal prohibition, child labour persists in India. Analyse causes and suggest solutions. (12.5 marks)

modules.module_name: Social Security, Labour Welfare and Emerging Vulnerabilities

topics.topic_name: Labour Laws, Gig Workers and Unorganised Sector
topics.primary_source: Code on Social Security 2020; Economic Survey Labour chapter; PLFS data
key_concepts: Labour code consolidation — 4 Codes replace 29 central labour laws
Code on Wages 2019 — universal minimum wage, equal remuneration
Code on Social Security 2020 — extends ESIC, EPFO to gig workers
Code on Industrial Relations 2020 — trade union recognition, retrenchment
Code on Occupational Safety 2020 — factory safety, contract labour
EPFO — Employees Provident Fund — retirement security for organised sector
ESIC — Employees State Insurance — health insurance for organised sector
PM-SYM — Pradhan Mantri Shram Yogi Maan-dhan — unorganised worker pension
e-Shram portal — national database of unorganised workers — 30 crore registered
Gig economy workers — Ola, Swiggy, Zomato — no employer-employee relationship
Gig workers rights — Rajasthan Platform-Based Gig Workers Act 2023 — first state law
MGNREGA as unorganised sector safety net — 7-8 crore households annually
BOCW Act 1996 — Building and Other Construction Workers welfare cess
Migrant workers — ISMW Act 1979 — inter-state migration protections
Minimum wages Act — state variation, non-compliance in agriculture

scheme_1.name: PM-SYM
scheme_1.objective: Rs 3000/month pension at 60 for unorganised workers earning <Rs 15000/month

scheme_2.name: e-Shram Portal
scheme_2.objective: Universal social security registration for unorganised workers

scheme_3.name: PMEGP
scheme_3.objective: Credit-linked subsidy for micro-enterprise creation in non-farm sector

issues_challenges: 90% of workforce in informal sector with no social security
Gig workers classified as independent contractors — no benefits
PM-SYM poor uptake — irregular income workers cannot commit to premium
BOCW cess — 50,000 crore collected, poorly utilised for workers
Portability of social security across states — migrant worker gap
reforms_needed: Portable social security accounts — follow worker across jobs/states
Mandatory social security for gig platforms proportionate to ride/order value
Universal pension — Rs 500/month minimum to all 60+ without pension
MGNREGA wage revision — link to CPI inflation
Enforce BOCW cess utilisation for construction workers' welfare

sample_questions: What challenges do gig workers face in India's social security framework? Discuss recent legislative responses. (12.5 marks)
Evaluate the consolidation of labour laws into four Codes. Are they worker-friendly? (10 marks)

daily_routine.reading: 1.5 hours — primary source + Economic Survey data
daily_routine.scheme_revision: 30 minutes — scheme name, year, objective flashcards

input_params.focus_area: welfare | gender | education | health | poverty | sc_st | child | labour

output_format.data_card: 3-5 statistics to remember for the topic
